title: Limiting Resources: Market-Led Reform and the Transformation of Public Goods By LaDawn Haglund Pennsylvania State University Press. 2010. 238 pages. $64.95 cloth, $29.95 paper
abstract: How has neoliberalism affected the ability of peripheral countries in the global economy to provide basic public goods like water and electricity? In a work that will be of special interest to sociologists of development, comparative-historical sociologists and political sociologists, LaDawn Haglund's book, Limiting Resources: Market-led Reform and the Transformation of Public Goods, explores this question through two insightful case studies of Costa Rica and El Salvador. The result of qualitatively-oriented fieldwork undertaken between 2002 and 2006, it combines original interviews and archival research of journalistic sources to examine changes in the institutional structure of the water and electricity sectors in these two, contrasting Central American countries over the past 30 years. Invoking Karl Polanyi's The Great Transformation (Beacon, 1944) as a theoretical flashpoint, the book examines how domestic ideological conflicts over the very definition of public goods had important consequences for state provision of water and electricity in these two countries. The book's central theoretical argument is that “multiple principles underlie public goods provision and that in order to provide public goods in ways that respond to these multiple ethics (including human rights and environmental sustainability), values other than cost and market efficiency must be included in conversations about reform.” (195) Haglund documents how, despite double movements against privatization that invoked precisely such values, political elites in both countries adopted minimalistic, market-oriented understandings of public goods as their own and went about changing policies and institutions to enact those ideologies (to varying effect). The introduction and first chapter of the book review key concepts in the political economy of public goods provision and argue that the “market-based organizing principles” underlying neoliberal understandings of public goods tend to crowd out other “organizing principles based on social justice.” Its middle chapters offer case-based accounts of the dissemination, adoption and institutionalization of market-oriented ideologies of public goods provision in the two countries, while the final chapter and conclusion offer theoretical implications.
